Affiliate of the Australian Labor Party

This article is about the current branch of the Australian Labor Party. For the period during the 1930s when the branch was briefly expelled from the Australian Labor Party, see [Lang Labor](/wiki/Lang_Labor "Lang Labor"). For the defunct left–wing Labor organisation that existed during the 1940s, see [State Labor Party](/wiki/State_Labor_Party "State Labor Party").

The **New South Wales Labor Party**, officially known as the **Australian Labor Party (New South Wales Branch)** and commonly referred to simply as **NSW Labor**, is the [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") branch of the [Australian Labor Party](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party "Australian Labor Party") (ALP). The branch is the current ruling party in the state of New South Wales and is led by [Chris Minns](/wiki/Chris_Minns "Chris Minns"), who has served concurrently as [premier of New South Wales](/wiki/Premier_of_New_South_Wales "Premier of New South Wales") since 2023.

The parliamentary leader is elected from and by the members of the [party caucus](/wiki/Caucus#In_Commonwealth_nations "Caucus"), comprising all party members in the [Legislative Assembly](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Legislative_Assembly "New South Wales Legislative Assembly") and [Legislative Council](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Legislative_Council "New South Wales Legislative Council"). The party factions have a strong influence on the election of the leader. The leader's position is dependent on the continuing support of the caucus (and party factions) and the leader may be deposed by failing to win a vote of confidence of parliamentary members. By convention, the premier sits in the Legislative Assembly, and is the leader of the party controlling a majority in that house. The party leader also typically is a member of the Assembly, though this is not a strict party constitutional requirement. [Barrie Unsworth](/wiki/Barrie_Unsworth "Barrie Unsworth"), for example, was elected party leader while a member of the Legislative Council. He then transferred to the Assembly by winning a seat at a by-election.

When the Labor party wins sufficient seats to be able to control a majority in the Legislative Assembly, the party leader becomes the [state Premier](/wiki/Premier_of_New_South_Wales "Premier of New South Wales") and Labor will form the [government](/wiki/Government_of_New_South_Wales "Government of New South Wales"). When Labor is the largest party not in government, the party leader becomes the [Leader of the Opposition](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Opposition_(New_South_Wales) "Leader of the Opposition (New South Wales)"). To become a premier or opposition leader, the party leader must be or within a short period of time become a member of the Legislative Assembly.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_South_Wales_Labor_Party&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

|  |  |

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_South_Wales_Labor_Party&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early history")]

The NSW branch of the Australian Labor Party, known as the **Labor Electoral League of New South Wales** from 1891 to 1917, first won 35 of the 141 seats in the NSW parliament at the [1891 election](/wiki/1891_New_South_Wales_colonial_election "1891 New South Wales colonial election"). The initial caucus voted against appointing a leader and the party was directed by a steering committee of five members until,[[b]](#cite_note-Committee-5) following a request from the party's extra-parliamentary executive, [Joseph Cook](/wiki/Joseph_Cook "Joseph Cook") was elected as the first leader in 1893. Cook left the party in the following year when he was obliged to sign a pledge that he would support all caucus decisions in parliament. [James McGowen](/wiki/James_McGowen "James McGowen"), who signed the pledge, succeeded Cook as party leader in 1894. At the [1894 state election](/wiki/1894_New_South_Wales_colonial_election "1894 New South Wales colonial election") Labor representation was reduced to 18. After the [1898 election](/wiki/1898_New_South_Wales_colonial_election "1898 New South Wales colonial election"), Labor held the balance of power with [George Reid](/wiki/George_Reid "George Reid")'s [Free Trade](/wiki/Free_Trade_Party "Free Trade Party") government being dependent on Labor to push through New South Wales' adoption of [Federation](/wiki/Federation_of_Australia "Federation of Australia"). McGowen's support for Federation was critical to Labor maintaining its support for the adoption of measures to implement Federation, even though the party remained opposed to the adopted Constitution, which it saw as biased in favour of business interests. The [1904 state election](/wiki/1904_New_South_Wales_state_election "1904 New South Wales state election") saw Labor become the second largest party and the official opposition for the first time.[[4]](#cite_note-Hogan-6)

### First government in New South Wales and internal divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_South_Wales_Labor_Party&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: First government in New South Wales and internal divisions")]

At the 1910 election, the Labor Party first won government in NSW with a slim majority of 46 of 90 seats; as a result, McGowen was premier from 1910 to 1913. But increasing success was accompanied by increasing tensions within the labour movement: from the 1890s the [Australian Workers' Union](/wiki/Australian_Workers%27_Union "Australian Workers' Union") attempted to assert its influence on the NSW parliamentary party, with little success for many years. Divisions also opened up between state and federal Labor after [Andrew Fisher](/wiki/Andrew_Fisher "Andrew Fisher") took office as Prime Minister of the [first federal Labor government](/wiki/First_Fisher_Ministry "First Fisher Ministry") in 1908: [William Holman](/wiki/William_Holman "William Holman"), the NSW party's main parliamentary strategist at the time, presented a policy agenda at state party conferences which differed from that approved by the federal party, in particular in its opposition to the transfer of responsibilities for finance and industrial relations from the state to the Commonwealth government, a dispute in which the AWU sided with the ALP. Tensions continued to rise during McGowen's premiership, as McGowen and Holman refused to support the holding of the [1911 trade and commerce referendum](/wiki/1911_Australian_referendum_(Trade_and_Commerce) "1911 Australian referendum (Trade and Commerce)") favoured by the ALP: in addition Holman alienated extra-parliamentary Labor members and some of the NSW caucus by appointing a number of new members to the state's [Legislative Council](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Legislative_Council "New South Wales Legislative Council"), including some who were not Labor members. This ran counter to the state party's platform, which advocated for the abolition of the Council, something which was not attempted by the state Labor governments of the 1910s, and which was a divisive issue in the party at the time.[[4]](#cite_note-Hogan-6)

McGowen was deposed by his deputy Holman after McGowen attempted to break a gas workers' strike by threatening to replace strikers with non-union labour. Upon the outbreak of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), Holman announced a truce with the state [Commonwealth Liberal Party](/wiki/Commonwealth_Liberal_Party "Commonwealth Liberal Party") which would take industrial reform off the agenda for the remainder of the war. In addition, the prospect of putting the economy on a war footing increased fears regarding job losses. These developments increased disillusion with Labor among unions and encouraged thinking about new political strategies. In November 1915 Labor affiliated unions in NSW, led by the AWU, formed the Industrial Section, the first formally organised Labor faction in Australia, with the aim of asserting control over the NSW parliamentary party. At the state party conference in April 1916, the Industrial Section, swept the party's internal elections, taking 31 out of 36 offices including the party's presidency. The Industrial Section became a model for later factional organisations in the ALP, both in New South Wales and nationwide.[[4]](#cite_note-Hogan-6)

### Conscription split

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_South_Wales_Labor_Party&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Conscription split")]

The [conscription](/wiki/Australian_Conscription "Australian Conscription") issue divided the Labor Party and wider Australian community in 1916. While much of the Australian labour movement and general community was opposed to conscription, Australian Labor Prime Minister [Billy Hughes](/wiki/Billy_Hughes "Billy Hughes") and Premier Holman strongly supported conscription, and both crossed the floor to vote with the conservative parties, and both were expelled from the Labor Party. When Hughes met with the executive of NSW Labor in September 1916 to attempt to persuade them to back his conscription plan, he lost the vote 21–5 and was warned that he would be expelled if he continued to press the matter.[[4]](#cite_note-Hogan-6) [Ernest Durack](/wiki/Ernest_Durack "Ernest Durack") became state party leader, while Holman formed a coalition on 15 November 1916 with the leader of the opposition [Liberal Reform Party](/wiki/Liberal_Reform_Party_(Australia) "Liberal Reform Party (Australia)"), [Charles Wade](/wiki/Charles_Wade "Charles Wade"), with himself as Premier. Following the exodus of pro-conscription MPs from the party, many leaders of the Industrial Section took advantage of the new vacancies to secure selection for open seats.[[4]](#cite_note-Hogan-6) Early in 1917, Holman and his supporters merged with Liberal Reform to form the state branch of the [Nationalist Party of Australia](/wiki/Nationalist_Party_of_Australia "Nationalist Party of Australia"). While the merged party was dominated by former Liberals, Holman became its leader. At the 1917 state election, the Nationalists won a huge victory, presaging the equally massive federal Nationalist victory later that year.

During his leadership of the Nationalist government, Holman vigorously defended the government-owned enterprises from the more conservative wing of his party. Durack's leadership lasted only for about three months, and he was succeeded by [John Storey](/wiki/John_Storey_(politician) "John Storey (politician)") in February 1917. In April 1918 the Industrial Section changed its name to the Industrial Vigilance Council, a change in part prompted by a leftward shift in the union movement influenced by the Great War and the [Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution"). At this point it was increasingly beset by internal divisions, in particular between the relatively conservative AWU and smaller unions and radicals such as the [syndicalist](/wiki/Syndicalist "Syndicalist")-influenced [Sam Rosa](/wiki/Sam_Rosa "Sam Rosa"). This came to a head during 1919 due to divisions over whether conscription should end following the closing of the First World War and whether the Australian union movement should adopt the syndicalist principle of the [One Big Union](/wiki/One_Big_Union_(concept) "One Big Union (concept)"). The faction was wound up in August 1919, with many of its radicals such as [Albert Willis](/wiki/Albert_Willis_(Australian_politician) "Albert Willis (Australian politician)") going on to form the [Industrial Socialist Labor Party](/wiki/Industrial_Socialist_Labor_Party "Industrial Socialist Labor Party"). This left the state party firmly in the control of the AWU[[4]](#cite_note-Hogan-6) At the 1920 election, Holman and his Nationalists were thrown from office in a massive swing to Labor. Storey became premier with a majority of one.

### Dooley–Storey era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_South_Wales_Labor_Party&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Dooley–Storey era")]

On Storey's death in October 1921, [James Dooley](/wiki/James_Dooley_(New_South_Wales_politician) "James Dooley (New South Wales politician)") became leader of the party and premier. His government was defeated on the floor of the House on 13 December 1921, but new Premier [George Fuller](/wiki/George_Fuller_(Australian_politician) "George Fuller (Australian politician)") lost a vote within seven hours of his appointment, and Dooley regained power. He lost the [1922 election](/wiki/1922_New_South_Wales_state_election "1922 New South Wales state election") to Fuller in a highly sectarian election campaign.[[5]](#cite_note-adb-7) As the result of a dispute with a party executive, dominated by the [Australian Workers' Union](/wiki/Australian_Workers%27_Union "Australian Workers' Union"), Dooley was expelled from the party in February 1923 and replaced by [Greg McGirr](/wiki/Greg_McGirr "Greg McGirr") as leader, but the [Federal Executive](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party_National_Executive "Australian Labor Party National Executive") intervened and appointed [Bill Dunn](/wiki/Bill_Dunn_(Australian_politician) "Bill Dunn (Australian politician)") as an interim leader until [Jack Lang](/wiki/Jack_Lang_(Australian_politician) "Jack Lang (Australian politician)") was elected by the caucus,[[6]](#cite_note-8) with the support of more radical unions, most prominently Albert Willis and his [Australian Coal and Shale Employees' Federation](/wiki/Australian_Coal_and_Shale_Employees%27_Federation "Australian Coal and Shale Employees' Federation").[[4]](#cite_note-Hogan-6)

### Lang era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_South_Wales_Labor_Party&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Lang era")]

Main article: [Lang Labor](/wiki/Lang_Labor "Lang Labor")

Lang led the ALP to victory in the [1925 election](/wiki/1925_New_South_Wales_state_election "1925 New South Wales state election") and became Premier. His support in the caucus was challenged in 1926 and in that year the party's annual State Conference, which strongly supported Lang, assumed the right to select the leader instead of caucus. The following year Lang and his extra-parliamentary allies drastically altered the party rules so that State Conference delegates and members of the Central Executive were elected in a complicated group system.[[7]](#cite_note-9) The ALP was defeated at the [1927 election](/wiki/1927_New_South_Wales_state_election "1927 New South Wales state election") but won in a landslide at the [1930 election](/wiki/1930_New_South_Wales_state_election "1930 New South Wales state election").

Lang opposed the [Premiers' Plan](/wiki/Premiers%27_Plan "Premiers' Plan") to combat the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") agreed to by the federal Labor government of [James Scullin](/wiki/James_Scullin "James Scullin") and the other state Premiers, who called for even more stringent cuts to government spending to balance the budget. In March 1931, the NSW branch of the party was expelled by the [Federal Executive](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party_National_Executive "Australian Labor Party National Executive") in the [Federal Conference](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party_National_Conference "Australian Labor Party National Conference").

In October 1931, Lang's followers in the federal House of Representatives crossed the floor to vote with the conservative [United Australia Party](/wiki/United_Australia_Party "United Australia Party") and bring down the Scullin government.

This action split the NSW Labor Party in two – Lang's followers and the expelled NSW branch became known as [Lang Labor](/wiki/Lang_Labor "Lang Labor"), while Scullin's supporters, led by Chifley, became known in NSW as [Federal Labor](/wiki/Federal_Labor_Party_(NSW) "Federal Labor Party (NSW)"). Most of the party's branches and affiliated trade unions supported Lang. Furthermore, Lang's persistence with his plan led to the [Lang Dismissal Crisis](/wiki/Lang_Dismissal_Crisis "Lang Dismissal Crisis") in 1931–32 which led to his dismissal as premier by the State Governor on 13 May 1932. The Governor appointed the UAP leader, [Bertram Stevens](/wiki/Bertram_Stevens_(politician) "Bertram Stevens (politician)"), as premier and Stevens immediately called the [1932 election](/wiki/1932_New_South_Wales_state_election "1932 New South Wales state election"), at which Labor was heavily defeated. In February 1936, the NSW branch rejoined the Australian Labor Party and became the official NSW branch of the ALP again.[[8]](#cite_note-langbio-10) Federal Labor was then abolished.

Lang's lack of success at state elections eroded his support within the labour movement. He had not won a state election since 1930. This led some members of caucus, including [Bob Heffron](/wiki/Bob_Heffron "Bob Heffron"), to break away to form the [Industrial Labor Party](/wiki/Industrial_Labor_Party "Industrial Labor Party"). In 1939, following intervention by the Federal Executive, the two factions were reunited at a state conference. This gathering also reversed the "red rules" and returned the power of selecting the party leader to the caucus. Lang was deposed in 1939.

### McKell and post-war era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_South_Wales_Labor_Party&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: McKell and post-war era")]

[William McKell](/wiki/William_McKell "William McKell") became party leader, reuniting and rejuvenating the party. Under his leadership the extreme left wing of the party had been expelled and had contested the [1941 election](/wiki/1941_New_South_Wales_state_election "1941 New South Wales state election") as the far left wing [State Labor Party](/wiki/State_Labor_Party "State Labor Party"). McKell led Labor to a convincing victory and became Premier. State Labor's poor showing had resulted in its dissolution shortly after the election. During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") McKell became a close collaborator of Labor Prime Ministers [John Curtin](/wiki/John_Curtin "John Curtin") and [Ben Chifley](/wiki/Ben_Chifley "Ben Chifley"), being a particularly close friend of the latter. Labor unity was again threatened by Jack Lang who had been expelled from the Labor Party in 1943 and formed another version of the [Lang Labor](/wiki/Lang_Labor "Lang Labor") Party. On this occasion he received no support from the rest of the caucus and spent the rest of the term as the sole member. At the [1944 election](/wiki/1944_New_South_Wales_state_election "1944 New South Wales state election") McKell won another victory, the first time a New South Wales Labor government had been re-elected. On early 1947 he resigned and announced acceptance of appointment as [Governor General](/wiki/Governor_General_of_Australia "Governor General of Australia").[[9]](#cite_note-mckinst-11) [James McGirr](/wiki/James_McGirr "James McGirr") was elected leader and premier and led Labor to another victory at the [1947 election](/wiki/1947_New_South_Wales_state_election "1947 New South Wales state election"). McGirr nearly lost the [1950 election](/wiki/1950_New_South_Wales_state_election "1950 New South Wales state election") and was replaced in 1952 by [Joseph Cahill](/wiki/Joseph_Cahill "Joseph Cahill").

In August 1953, two [Fairfield](/wiki/Fairfield_City_Council "Fairfield City Council") aldermen − including a former [mayor](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Fairfield "List of mayors of Fairfield") − split from ALP to form the [Official Labour Movement](/wiki/Official_Labour_Movement "Official Labour Movement").[[10]](#cite_note-12) Although it won two seats at the [local government elections in December 1953](/w/index.php?title=1953_New_South_Wales_local_elections&action=edit&redlink=1 "1953 New South Wales local elections (page does not exist)"), it failed to defeat the ALP at state or federal elections and faded away soon after.[[11]](#cite_note-13)[[12]](#cite_note-14)

### Labor in government, 1952–1965

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_South_Wales_Labor_Party&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Labor in government, 1952–1965")]

Cahill decisively won the [1953 election](/wiki/1953_New_South_Wales_state_election "1953 New South Wales state election"). He was desperate to keep the New South Wales branch of the ALP united despite the sectarian and ideological split that resulted in the formation of the right-wing [Democratic Labor Party](/wiki/Democratic_Labour_Party_(Australia,_1980) "Democratic Labour Party (Australia, 1980)") in 1954. He achieved this by controlling the anti-DLP faction in his party. The DLP did not contest the [1956 election](/wiki/1956_New_South_Wales_state_election "1956 New South Wales state election"), which Labor won. Cahill was returned in the [1959 election](/wiki/1959_New_South_Wales_state_election "1959 New South Wales state election"), but died in office later that year. He was succeeded as leader and premier by [Bob Heffron](/wiki/Bob_Heffron "Bob Heffron"). Heffron continued the Labor reign in New South Wales winning the [1962 election](/wiki/1962_New_South_Wales_state_election "1962 New South Wales state election"). Heffron resigned the leadership and premiership in 1964, and was succeeded by [Jack Renshaw](/wiki/Jack_Renshaw "Jack Renshaw"), who lost the premiership at the [1965 election](/wiki/1965_New_South_Wales_state_election "1965 New South Wales state election") ending 24 years of Labor power in the state.

### Opposition, 1965–1976

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_South_Wales_Labor_Party&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Opposition, 1965–1976")]

Renshaw also lost the [1968 election](/wiki/1968_New_South_Wales_state_election "1968 New South Wales state election"), after which he resigned the leadership, to be succeeded by [Pat Hills](/wiki/Pat_Hills "Pat Hills"). Hills lost the [1971](/wiki/1971_New_South_Wales_state_election "1971 New South Wales state election") and [1973 election](/wiki/1973_New_South_Wales_state_election "1973 New South Wales state election") after which he was deposed by [Neville Wran](/wiki/Neville_Wran "Neville Wran").

### Wran–Unsworth era, 1976–1988

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_South_Wales_Labor_Party&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Wran–Unsworth era, 1976–1988")]

Wran narrowly won the [1976 election](/wiki/1976_New_South_Wales_state_election "1976 New South Wales state election") and remained premier until 1986, winning two [Landslide victory](/wiki/Landslide_victory "Landslide victory")'s popularly known as the "Wranslide." In [1978 election](/wiki/1978_New_South_Wales_state_election "1978 New South Wales state election") and [1981 election](/wiki/1981_New_South_Wales_state_election "1981 New South Wales state election"). He was succeeded by [Barrie Unsworth](/wiki/Barrie_Unsworth "Barrie Unsworth") who took over the premiership until Labor's loss at the [1988 election](/wiki/1988_New_South_Wales_state_election "1988 New South Wales state election"), after which he resigned.

### Carr era, 1988–2005

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_South_Wales_Labor_Party&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Carr era, 1988–2005")]

[Bob Carr](/wiki/Bob_Carr "Bob Carr") became leader in 1988 and led Labor to victory in the [1995 election](/wiki/1995_New_South_Wales_state_election "1995 New South Wales state election"). Carr was premier for 10 years, before resigning in 2005. He was the longest continuous-serving premier in New South Wales.

### Iemma era, 2005–2008

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_South_Wales_Labor_Party&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Iemma era, 2005–2008")]

See also: [Sydney Metro (2008 proposal)](/wiki/Sydney_Metro_(2008_proposal) "Sydney Metro (2008 proposal)")

Carr was succeeded by [Morris Iemma](/wiki/Morris_Iemma "Morris Iemma"). At the time, the state transport network was inferior, and public transport was often criticised as the most stark example of Carr's inaction.[[13]](#cite_note-15) Iemma and the [Treasurer](/wiki/Treasurer_of_New_South_Wales "Treasurer of New South Wales") [Michael Costa](/wiki/Michael_Costa_(politician) "Michael Costa (politician)") supported the idea to sidestep the high costs and industrial risks of the existing RailCorp network and instead begin building a new rapid transit rail system in parallel. The new system would incorporate smaller, lighter rolling stock, reducing construction costs, and be operated by the private sector, reducing operating costs. But despite the savings available via the rapid transit option, the costs were still beyond the state's means.

Iemma comfortably won the [2007 election](/wiki/2007_New_South_Wales_state_election "2007 New South Wales state election") with the loss of only four seats. His strategy was to draw a line under the failures of the Carr era and ask the electorate for another chance. The Opposition ran a poor campaign, and the electorate was minded to trust the affable, hard-working new premier. Iemma was returned but with far less political capital than the size of Labor's majority would suggest.[[14]](#cite_note-betrayal-16)

#### Proposed electricity privatisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_South_Wales_Labor_Party&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Proposed electricity privatisation")]

> This is why I am the Premier.

— Morris Iemma, late 2007

Journalist Simon Benson describes a crucial meeting in late 2007 between Iemma and Labor state president [Bernie Riordan](/w/index.php?title=Bernie_Riordan&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bernie Riordan (page does not exist)") during the height of the furore over electricity privatisation:

> [Iemma] had laid out on his desk a spreadsheet of infrastructure projects … On the bottom half were all the projects the state needed if it was to avoid choking on its own congestion within the next decade. It amounted to more than $25 billion. And that was what they hadn't even announced.  
>   
> "This is why I am the Premier," he told Riordan, emphasising his belief that it was critical for the privatisation to succeed. "These [projects] need to be done. I need to do these."[[14]](#cite_note-betrayal-16)

Immediately following the 2007 election, Iemma and [Treasurer](/wiki/Treasurer_of_New_South_Wales "Treasurer of New South Wales") [Costa](/wiki/Michael_Costa_(politician) "Michael Costa (politician)") secretly plotted their strategy for the next four years, under the heading of "bullets to bite". Both knew that if they were to build the new rapid transit system and address the growing crisis in electricity supply, they would need to bring the private sector into the state's electricity industry first.[[14]](#cite_note-betrayal-16)

The federal party's immediate political needs intervened, with the new federal Labor leader [Kevin Rudd](/wiki/Kevin_Rudd "Kevin Rudd") persuading Iemma to defer his privatisation announcement until after the [2007 federal election](/wiki/2007_Australian_federal_election "2007 Australian federal election"). Rudd felt he could ill afford the distraction of a debate over asset sales in the country's most populous state. With the funding source a secret, the rapid transit plan had to be kept under wraps as well.[[14]](#cite_note-betrayal-16)[[15]](#cite_note-17)

Along with his plan to sell the government's electricity generation and retailing companies, Iemma announced a massive infrastructure scheme involving [South West Rail Link](/wiki/South_West_Rail_Link "South West Rail Link"), an inner city motorway network, and the [Metro Link network](/wiki/Sydney_Metro_(2008_proposal)#Metro_Link "Sydney Metro (2008 proposal)"), a system of underground, privately operated, single-deck, automated trains. The government planned to use up to $3 billion from the sale of retail electricity to help fund the inner city motorway network, linking the [M4 Western Motorway](/wiki/M4_Motorway_(Sydney) "M4 Motorway (Sydney)") to the [CBD](/wiki/Sydney_central_business_district "Sydney central business district") and the [airport](/wiki/Sydney_Airport "Sydney Airport")[[16]](#cite_note-18)

The [Electrical Trades Union](/wiki/Electrical_Trades_Union_of_Australia "Electrical Trades Union of Australia") (ETU) was ideologically opposed to the privatisation of the electricity businesses and led opposition to the plan. The ETU's secretary, Bernie Riordan, was also the president of NSW Labor. The secretary of the [Unions NSW](/wiki/Labor_Council_of_New_South_Wales "Labor Council of New South Wales"), [John Robertson](/wiki/John_Robertson_(New_South_Wales_politician) "John Robertson (New South Wales politician)"), was also a member of the ETU and opposed the privatisation.[[17]](#cite_note-19) The party secretary, [Karl Bitar](/wiki/Karl_Bitar "Karl Bitar"), and his deputy, [Luke Foley](/wiki/Luke_Foley "Luke Foley"), were less ideologically committed on the issue, but their polling showed that the public was against a sale and – more importantly – tiring of Iemma and his team. They too came down against Iemma's plan.[[14]](#cite_note-betrayal-16)

What followed was an extraordinary, year-long struggle by the party's head office and affiliated trade unions to force cabinet and caucus to abandon an already-announced policy. Robertson, Bitar and Foley had the union-dominated party conference pass a motion reminding MPs of the party's notional commitment to [democratic socialism](/wiki/Democratic_socialism "Democratic socialism"). Next, union and party officials began threatening Labor MPs with disendorsement if they voted with the Premier on privatisation. Remembering the promise Rudd had made to him in 2007, Iemma called on the Prime Minister to have the federal party intervene to protect MPs who sided with the Government. Rudd declined. Former Prime Minister [Paul Keating](/wiki/Paul_Keating "Paul Keating"), former premiers Carr, [Barrie Unsworth](/wiki/Barrie_Unsworth "Barrie Unsworth") and [Neville Wran](/wiki/Neville_Wran "Neville Wran"), and former Labor Council secretaries [Michael Easson](/wiki/Michael_Easson "Michael Easson") and [John McBean](/w/index.php?title=John_McBean&action=edit&redlink=1 "John McBean (page does not exist)") came out publicly in support of Iemma.[[14]](#cite_note-betrayal-16)

On 3 May 2008, the NSW Labor's State Conference rejected, by 702 to 107 votes, the Iemma government's plans to privatise the state's electricity system.[[18]](#cite_note-20)[[19]](#cite_note-21)

Two members of the party's [Socialist Left faction](/wiki/Socialist_Left_(Australia) "Socialist Left (Australia)"), upper house MPs [Lynda Voltz](/wiki/Lynda_Voltz "Lynda Voltz") and [Ian West](/wiki/Ian_West_(Australian_politician) "Ian West (Australian politician)"), succumbed to the pressure and announced that they would vote against the party on privatisation.[[20]](#cite_note-22) Had the vote gone ahead, it would have been the first instance of a Labor MP '[crossing the floor](/wiki/Crossing_the_floor "Crossing the floor")' in 14 years of government. The [Liberal](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Liberal_Party "New South Wales Liberal Party") Opposition, led by moderate [Barry O'Farrell](/wiki/Barry_O%27Farrell "Barry O'Farrell"), saw an opportunity to wound Iemma by denying him the opportunity to build the metro. Together, West, Voltz, the [Greens](/wiki/The_Greens_NSW "The Greens NSW") and the Opposition had the numbers to defeat the Government in the upper house in August 2008.

On 3 September 2008, [Deputy Premier](/wiki/Deputy_Premier_of_New_South_Wales "Deputy Premier of New South Wales") and Transport Minister [John Watkins](/wiki/John_Watkins_(Australian_politician) "John Watkins (Australian politician)") announced he was going to retire for family reasons, triggering a cabinet reshuffle.[[21]](#cite_note-23) On 4 September 2008, Costa was advised by Iemma that he would no longer be in the forthcoming reshuffle and hence dumped as Treasurer.[[22]](#cite_note-24) Iemma had also proposed that other ministers would be dumped, including Health Minister [Reba Meagher](/wiki/Reba_Meagher "Reba Meagher"). Iemma's faction, Centre Unity, supported the sacking of the Treasurer but not the other four Ministers. The following day, right-wing Labor powerbrokers [Eddie Obeid](/wiki/Eddie_Obeid "Eddie Obeid") and [Joe Tripodi](/wiki/Joe_Tripodi "Joe Tripodi") told Iemma that he had lost the support of MPs and would not survive a caucus meeting. [Nathan Rees](/wiki/Nathan_Rees "Nathan Rees") also walked into the meeting with a number of Labor MPs announcing he had the numbers to overthrow Iemma. Seeing that he had lost the support of his caucus faction, Iemma resigned as leader and Premier.[[23]](#cite_note-25)[[24]](#cite_note-26) He was replaced by Rees as leader and Premier.[[25]](#cite_note-27)

### Rees and Keneally era, 2008–2011

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_South_Wales_Labor_Party&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Rees and Keneally era, 2008–2011")]

Rees was leader and premier for only 15 months before he was deposed by [Kristina Keneally](/wiki/Kristina_Keneally "Kristina Keneally"), who resigned after Labor was defeated in a landslide at the [2011 election](/wiki/2011_New_South_Wales_state_election "2011 New South Wales state election").

### Opposition, 2011–2023

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_South_Wales_Labor_Party&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Opposition, 2011–2023")]

Keneally was succeeded by [John Robertson](/wiki/John_Robertson_(politician,_born_1962) "John Robertson (politician, born 1962)"). He resigned in December 2014, after the [2014 Sydney hostage crisis](/wiki/2014_Sydney_hostage_crisis "2014 Sydney hostage crisis"), after it was revealed that he had had contact with [Man Haron Monis](/wiki/Man_Haron_Monis "Man Haron Monis"), who was one of Robertson's constituents. On 5 January 2015 [Luke Foley](/wiki/Luke_Foley "Luke Foley") was elected leader. In the [2015 state election](/wiki/2015_New_South_Wales_state_election "2015 New South Wales state election"), Labor achieved a 9.9-point two-party-preferred swing, but the Coalition comfortably retained government. Foley resigned in November 2018 in the face of sexual assault allegations, and was succeeded by [Michael Daley](/wiki/Michael_Daley "Michael Daley") in the resulting [leadership contest](/wiki/2018_Australian_Labor_Party_(New_South_Wales_Branch)_leadership_election "2018 Australian Labor Party (New South Wales Branch) leadership election"). In the [2019 election](/wiki/2019_New_South_Wales_state_election "2019 New South Wales state election"), the party recorded a small TPP swing in its favour and won two seats, but remained in opposition. On 25 March 2019, Daley announced his intention to step down as leader. [Penny Sharpe](/wiki/Penny_Sharpe "Penny Sharpe"), who was elected deputy leader in November 2018, served as interim leader until the [leadership ballot](/wiki/2019_Australian_Labor_Party_(New_South_Wales_Branch)_leadership_election "2019 Australian Labor Party (New South Wales Branch) leadership election") was held in June; [Jodi McKay](/wiki/Jodi_McKay "Jodi McKay") was elected leader. In May 2021, McKay resigned the leadership, and was replaced by [Chris Minns](/wiki/Chris_Minns "Chris Minns") on 4 June 2021.[[26]](#cite_note-28)

### Minns era, 2021–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_South_Wales_Labor_Party&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Minns era, 2021–present")]

Further information: [Minns shadow ministry, 2021–2023](/wiki/Shadow_Ministry_of_Chris_Minns "Shadow Ministry of Chris Minns"); [2023 state election](/wiki/2023_New_South_Wales_state_election "2023 New South Wales state election"); and [Minns ministry, 2023–present](/wiki/Minns_ministry "Minns ministry")

#### 2021–2023

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_South_Wales_Labor_Party&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: 2021–2023")]

After going through five leaders since the [2011 state election](/wiki/2011_New_South_Wales_state_election "2011 New South Wales state election"), which included four leadership elections ([2015](/wiki/2015_New_South_Wales_Labor_Party_leadership_election "2015 New South Wales Labor Party leadership election"), [2018](/wiki/2018_New_South_Wales_Labor_Party_leadership_election "2018 New South Wales Labor Party leadership election"), [2019](/wiki/2019_New_South_Wales_Labor_Party_leadership_election "2019 New South Wales Labor Party leadership election"), [2021](/wiki/2021_New_South_Wales_Labor_Party_leadership_election "2021 New South Wales Labor Party leadership election")), New South Wales Labor, led by [Leader of the Opposition](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Opposition_(New_South_Wales) "Leader of the Opposition (New South Wales)") and [right-faction](/wiki/Labor_Right "Labor Right") MP for [Kogarah](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Kogarah "Electoral district of Kogarah") [Chris Minns](/wiki/Chris_Minns "Chris Minns"),[[27]](#cite_note-29) won back the state after 11 years and 362 days in opposition. Minns had been leader of the party since [June 2021](/wiki/2021_New_South_Wales_Labor_Party_leadership_election "2021 New South Wales Labor Party leadership election") after being elected unopposed following the resignation of former leader [Jodi McKay](/wiki/Jodi_McKay "Jodi McKay"). Minns announced his [shadow ministry](/wiki/Shadow_Ministry_of_Chris_Minns "Shadow Ministry of Chris Minns") over the following week, with a "focus on working families, [Western Sydney](/wiki/Western_Sydney "Western Sydney") and the cost-of-living..."[[28]](#cite_note-30) Months after becoming leader of the party Minns reiterated his party's plan to invest in [Western Sydney](/wiki/Western_Sydney "Western Sydney") after stating at the Labor Party state conference that his party would scrap the [Beaches Link](/wiki/Beaches_Link "Beaches Link") and redirect the funds ($10 billion) to public transport infrastructure to Greater Western Sydney.[[29]](#cite_note-31)[[c]](#cite_note-34) He also criticised the privatisation of [WestConnex](/wiki/WestConnex "WestConnex"), stating: "Look at the circumstances surrounding the privatisation of WestConnex... just one road as part of the WestConnex network will make $28 billion from the pockets of the families of western Sydney".[[32]](#cite_note-35)

By the time of the 2023 state election, the party had a myriad of key election promises, including: scrapping the public-sector wages cap;[[33]](#cite_note-36)[[34]](#cite_note-37) "Improve conditions for tenants by establishing a Rental Commissioner to advocate for renters, allowing renters to directly transfer bonds from one property to another, outlawing secret rental bidding, and listing reasonable grounds for owners to end a lease (to stop unfair evictions);"[[35]](#cite_note-Seg-38) "ensure the trains are built in New South Wales, creating at least 1,000 jobs;"[[36]](#cite_note-Cock-39) "establish TAFE manufacturing centres so every year, an extra 1,000 apprentices can be trained in skills like mechanical engineering and electrical fitting for free;"[[36]](#cite_note-Cock-39) "Tradies and truck drivers using the [M5 East](/wiki/M5_Motorway_(Sydney) "M5 Motorway (Sydney)") and [M8 would](/wiki/M8_Motorway_(Sydney) "M8 Motorway (Sydney)") also have their tolls cut. Those who take two trips a day, five days a week will save a total of $3,800 a year;"[[36]](#cite_note-Cock-39) and ban financial donations from clubs as well as trialing a mandatory cashless gaming card, among many others.[[35]](#cite_note-Seg-38)

#### 2023–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_South_Wales_Labor_Party&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: 2023–present")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_South_Wales_Labor_Party&action=edit&section=). *(September 2024)* |

In March 2023, the New South Wales Labor Party won the election, however only won a minority government.[[37]](#cite_note-40)[[38]](#cite_note-41) At the time Minns was appointed leader of the party, the [Premier of New South Wales](/wiki/Premier_of_New_South_Wales "Premier of New South Wales") was [Liberal Party](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Liberal_Party "New South Wales Liberal Party") leader [Gladys Berejiklian](/wiki/Gladys_Berejiklian "Gladys Berejiklian"). However, four months later Berejiklian was replaced by [Dominic Perrottet](/wiki/Dominic_Perrottet "Dominic Perrottet"). Initially opinion polls remained fairly steady, with the [Liberal](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Liberal_Party "New South Wales Liberal Party")–[National](/wiki/New_South_Wales_National_Party "New South Wales National Party") Coalition government losing one to two points.[[39]](#cite_note-42)[[40]](#cite_note-43) By September 2022 (1 year, 106 days after Minns became party leader) the party took the lead in the primary vote polls for the first time in over a decade,[[41]](#cite_note-44) and maintained the [two-party-preferred](/wiki/Two-party-preferred_vote "Two-party-preferred vote") opinion poll consistently until the [state election](/wiki/2023_New_South_Wales_state_election "2023 New South Wales state election"). *ABC News* stated, following the election, that "Labor's pledge to scrap the Coalition's public-sector wages cap was, in hindsight, instrumental in delivering the party majority government."[[42]](#cite_note-45)

Since coming into office the party has officially achieved two election commitments:[[43]](#cite_note-46) enshrining [Sydney Water](/wiki/Sydney_Water "Sydney Water") and [Hunter Water](/wiki/Hunter_Water "Hunter Water") as permanent public institutions via a [Constitutional](/wiki/Constitution_Act_1902 "Constitution Act 1902") [amendment](/wiki/Constitutional_amendment "Constitutional amendment");[[44]](#cite_note-47) raising the stamp duty exemption threshold for first-home buyers from $650k to $800k.[[45]](#cite_note-48)

## Attempted party reforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_South_Wales_Labor_Party&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Attempted party reforms")]

Between 2009 and 2014, the [Independent Commission Against Corruption](/wiki/Independent_Commission_Against_Corruption_(New_South_Wales) "Independent Commission Against Corruption (New South Wales)") (ICAC) began or completed a series of investigations into the behaviours of a number of Labor politicians, including [Angela D'Amore](/wiki/Angela_D%27Amore "Angela D'Amore"),[[46]](#cite_note-49) [Tony Kelly](/wiki/Tony_Kelly_(politician) "Tony Kelly (politician)"),[[47]](#cite_note-Aust-2011-12-13-50) [Ian Macdonald](/wiki/Ian_Macdonald_(New_South_Wales_politician) "Ian Macdonald (New South Wales politician)"),[[48]](#cite_note-51)[[49]](#cite_note-abc20130731-52)[[50]](#cite_note-53) [Eddie Obeid](/wiki/Eddie_Obeid "Eddie Obeid"),[[51]](#cite_note-ICACsmh2014-06-05-54)[[52]](#cite_note-ICACaust2014-06-05-55)[[53]](#cite_note-ICACabc2014-06-05-56) [Karyn Paluzzano](/wiki/Karyn_Paluzzano "Karyn Paluzzano"),[[54]](#cite_note-57)[[55]](#cite_note-58) and [Joe Tripodi](/wiki/Joe_Tripodi "Joe Tripodi").[[52]](#cite_note-ICACaust2014-06-05-55) The ICAC made a series of adverse findings against all six politicians, although Paluzzano was the only one to face criminal charges. For bringing the party into disrepute, Kelly had his membership of Labor terminated in 2011;[[56]](#cite_note-SMH-2011-12-12-59)[[57]](#cite_note-60) both Macdonald and Obeid had their membership terminated in 2013;[[58]](#cite_note-expell-61) and Tripodi suffered the same fate in 2014.[[52]](#cite_note-ICACaust2014-06-05-55)[[53]](#cite_note-ICACabc2014-06-05-56) Other investigations and criminal charges were laid against [Craig Thomson](/wiki/Craig_Thomson_(politician) "Craig Thomson (politician)"), a federal politician from New South Wales, and [Michael Williamson](/wiki/Michael_Williamson_(Australian_unionist) "Michael Williamson (Australian unionist)"), a senior Labor official, also from New South Wales. Both Thomson and Williamson were adversely implicated in the [Health Services Union expenses affair](/wiki/Health_Services_Union_expenses_affair "Health Services Union expenses affair"). Their membership of NSW Labor was terminated in 2014.[[59]](#cite_note-SMHexpel-62)[[60]](#cite_note-ABCexpel-63)

Seeking to stamp out perceived corruption and [factional](/wiki/Political_faction "Political faction") infighting, [Senator](/wiki/Australian_Senate "Australian Senate") [John Faulkner](/wiki/John_Faulkner "John Faulkner") began a process of reforms that proposed to include [rank–and–file](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/rank_and_file "wikt:rank and file") members in decisions such as the selection of candidates for Senate and Legislative Council vacancies and [party tickets](/wiki/Ticket_(election) "Ticket (election)"), and a vote in the direct election of the New South Wales parliamentary leaders.[[61]](#cite_note-64) However, Faulkner's reform proposals were mostly rejected at NSW Labor's 2014 conference.[[62]](#cite_note-65) The direct election of party leader gained support with effect from after the [2015 election](/wiki/2015_New_South_Wales_state_election "2015 New South Wales state election").[[63]](#cite_note-66)

Following the suspension of the general secretary of NSW Labor, [Kaila Murnain](/wiki/Kaila_Murnain "Kaila Murnain") in 2019, the NSW Labor Leader [Jodi McKay](/wiki/Jodi_McKay "Jodi McKay") and federal Labor Leader [Anthony Albanese](/wiki/Anthony_Albanese "Anthony Albanese") announced a further review into the party.[[64]](#cite_note-67) [Michael Lavarch](/wiki/Michael_Lavarch "Michael Lavarch") conducted the review after Murnain admitted to the [Independent Commission Against Corruption (New South Wales)](/wiki/Independent_Commission_Against_Corruption_(New_South_Wales) "Independent Commission Against Corruption (New South Wales)") that she was aware that billionaire property developer [Huang Xiangmo](/wiki/Huang_Xiangmo "Huang Xiangmo") made an illegal $100,000 donation to NSW Labor in 2016.[[65]](#cite_note-68) The Lavarch Review recommended changes to internal governance and oversight mechanisms within NSW Labor including the establishment of a State Executive Board to oversee the existing NSW Labor Administrative Committee.[[66]](#cite_note-69)

## Country Labor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_South_Wales_Labor_Party&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Country Labor")]

**Country Labor** was a subsection of the ALP, and was used as a designation by candidates contesting elections in rural areas. It functioned as a sort of [ginger group](/wiki/Ginger_group "Ginger group") within the party, and was somewhat analogous to its [youth wing](/wiki/Australian_Young_Labor "Australian Young Labor"). The **Country Labor Party** was registered as a separate party in NSW, until 2021[[67]](#cite_note-70) and was also registered with the [Australian Electoral Commission](/wiki/Australian_Electoral_Commission "Australian Electoral Commission") (AEC) for federal elections.[[68]](#cite_note-aec-71)

The creation of a separate designation for rural candidates was first suggested at the June 1999 ALP state conference in NSW. In May 2000, following Labor's success at the [2000 Benalla by-election](/wiki/2000_Benalla_state_by-election "2000 Benalla state by-election") in Victoria, [Kim Beazley](/wiki/Kim_Beazley "Kim Beazley") announced that the ALP intended to register a separate "Country Labor Party" with the AEC;[[69]](#cite_note-boland-72) this occurred in October 2000.[[68]](#cite_note-aec-71) The Country Labor designation is most frequently used in New South Wales. According to the ALP's financial statements for the 2015–16 financial year, NSW Country Labor had around 2,600 members (around 17 percent of the party total), but almost no assets. It recorded a severe funding shortfall at the [2015 NSW state election](/wiki/2015_New_South_Wales_state_election "2015 New South Wales state election"), and had to rely on a $1.68 million loan from the party proper to remain solvent. It had been initially assumed that the party proper could provide the money from its own resources, but the NSW Electoral Commission ruled that this was impermissible because the parties were registered separately. Instead the party proper had to loan Country Labor the required funds at a commercial interest rate.[[70]](#cite_note-73)

The dedicated Country Labor political party was de-registered in October 2021 in a major party reform.[[71]](#cite_note-countryderegister-74) Instead of a dedicated political party, the ALP instead pledged to dedicate "30 per cent of winnable seats in upper house ticket to people who live in regional NSW".[[71]](#cite_note-countryderegister-74)
